clean_story: Private redness.  How "you" feels to you. "Qualia" names the raw, subjective texture of experience—the way coffee’s bitterness tastes to you, or how a sunset’s crimson glows in your mind, not just on your retina. It’s like trying to describe the flavor of pineapple to someone who’s only read about fruit. You can list acids, sugars, enzymes—but the zap of it?That’s qualia. Here’s the mystery:Brain scans show neurons firing when you see blue, but they can’t measure blueness. A colorblind person’s "red" might match your "green" in wavelength—yet their qualia is unknowable. Philosopher Frank Jackson’s "Mary’s Room" thought experiment asks:If a scientist born in a black-and-white room learns everything about color vision—then sees red for the first time—does she learn something new?If yes, qualia isn’t just physical data. Some argue qualia are illusions—brain shortcuts for survival. Others say they’re proof consciousness can’t be fully explained by neurons and chemistry alone. Three related subjects are consciousness hard problem, solipsism limits and inverted spectrum.
